# Tools and Platforms {#sec-tools}

This chapter covers the software and services the lab uses for communication, collaboration, and research. For HPC-specific tools (Savio, Apptainer, SLURM), see the [High Performance Computing](#sec-hpc-overview) section.

## Slack

Slack is our primary communication tool. The workspace is organized into channels:

| Channel | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `#general` | Lab-wide announcements and casual conversation |
| `#computing` | Questions about Savio, software, debugging, and data |
| `#papers` | Interesting papers, reading group discussions |
| `#proj-<name>` | Project-specific channels (created as needed) |
| `#random` | Off-topic, social, fun |

### Norms

- Use threads to keep channels readable.
- Use `@here` and `@channel` sparingly — only when something is truly time-sensitive.
- It is okay to message on evenings or weekends if that is when you are working, but do not expect a response until the next business day.
- Search before asking — someone may have already answered your question.

## Google Workspace

### Shared Drive

The lab maintains a Google Shared Drive for non-code documents: meeting notes, slide decks, literature reviews, grant proposals, and partner communications. The recommended structure is:

```
Carleton Lab/
├── Admin/
├── Grants/
├── Lab meetings/
├── Projects/
│   └── <project-name>/
│       ├── Literature/
│       ├── Presentations/
│       └── Notes/
└── Templates/
```

### Calendar

Use your Berkeley Google Calendar for scheduling. When booking meetings with lab members, check their calendar availability first. Tamma's calendar is maintained by her — reach out via Slack or email to schedule time.

## GitHub

All analysis code is hosted on the lab's GitHub organization. See @sec-data-code for conventions around repositories, branching, and code review.

If you are new to Git, the following resources are excellent starting points:

- [Happy Git and GitHub for the useR](https://happygitwithr.com/) by Jenny Bryan
- [D-Lab Git Fundamentals](https://github.com/dlab-berkeley/git-fundamentals)
- [Software Carpentry Git lesson](https://swcarpentry.github.io/git-novice/)

## Reference management

We use [Zotero](https://www.zotero.org/) for managing references. The lab has a shared Zotero library. To get started:

1. Create a free Zotero account.
2. Install the Zotero desktop app and the browser connector.
3. Ask the lab manager to invite you to the shared library.

Zotero integrates with Google Docs, Word, and can export `.bib` files for use with LaTeX and Quarto.

## Writing tools

Lab members use a variety of writing tools depending on the project:

- **LaTeX / Overleaf** for journal submissions and working papers. Most economics journals expect LaTeX-formatted manuscripts.
- **Quarto** for reproducible reports, presentations, and documentation (like this manual).
- **Google Docs** for early-stage drafts, collaborative editing with non-technical partners, and internal memos.

## IDEs and editors

Use whatever editor you are most productive in. Common choices in the lab:

- **Positron** — a data-science-focused IDE from Posit (makers of RStudio), with strong R and Python support and an integrated terminal. Works well with Remote SSH for Savio.
- **RStudio** — the classic choice for R-heavy workflows.
- **VS Code** — highly extensible, excellent for Python, and supports Remote SSH natively.

For connecting any of these to Savio, see @sec-remote-ssh.
